[Versions Notes](https://www.mdpi.com/1424-8220/24/2/469/notes)

Abstract
--------

Recent earthquakes worldwide have led to significant loss of life and structural damage to infrastructure, especially road bridges. Existing bridge monitoring systems have limitations, including restricted detection capabilities, subjectivity, human error, labor-intensive inspections, limited access to remote areas, and high costs. Aging infrastructures pose a critical concern for organizations and government funding policies, showing signs of decay and impending structural failure. To address these challenges, this research proposes an IoT-based bridge health status monitoring and warning system that is wireless, low-cost, durable, and user-friendly. The proposed system builds upon engineering standards and guidelines to classify bridge health status into categories ranging from excellent to collapse condition. It incorporates deflection, vibration, temperature, humidity, and infrared sensors, combined with IoT and a fuzzy logic algorithm. The primary objective is to reduce bridge maintenance costs, extend lifespans, and enhance transportation safety through an early warning system via a mobile application. Additionally, a Google Maps interface has been developed to display bridge conditions along with real-time traffic video. To validate the proposed system, a 3-D prototype model was constructed and tested. Practical testing of the fuzzy logic algorithm aligned with the simulation outcomes, demonstrating expected accuracy in determining bridge health status.

Keywords: 

[resilient infrastructure](https://www.mdpi.com/search?q=resilient+infrastructure); [fuzzy logic](https://www.mdpi.com/search?q=fuzzy+logic); [intelligent transport systems](https://www.mdpi.com/search?q=intelligent+transport+systems); [internet of things](https://www.mdpi.com/search?q=internet+of+things); [smart road bridges](https://www.mdpi.com/search?q=smart+road+bridges); [structural health monitoring systems](https://www.mdpi.com/search?q=structural+health+monitoring+systems); [UNSDGs](https://www.mdpi.com/search?q=UNSDGs)

1. Introduction
---------------

Road bridge failures can have severe ramifications on transportation systems worldwide. Apart from the loss of lives and casualties, the disruption in services leads to highly adverse effects on economic growth. Throughout history, numerous bridge failures have resulted in tragic consequences, including human fatalities and substantial economic losses. A recent example is the collapse of Italy’s Morandi’s Polcevera Viaduct in 2018, resulting in 43 deaths and nine injuries as well as an estimated economic loss of approximately 100 million yuan (approximately 14 million dollars) [[1](https://www.mdpi.com/1424-8220/24/2/469#B1-sensors-24-00469)].

In the United States, according to the latest Report Card for America’s Infrastructure, 42% of the 617,000 country’s bridges are at least 50 years old; among these, 7.5% fall under the classification of “structurally deficient” [[2](https://www.mdpi.com/1424-8220/24/2/469#B2-sensors-24-00469)]. 

China, a country with massive bridge production, had a total number of 851,500 bridges by the end of 2018 [[3](https://www.mdpi.com/1424-8220/24/2/469#B3-sensors-24-00469)]. Despite this extensive infrastructure, the country has encountered several serious bridge collapses [[4](https://www.mdpi.com/1424-8220/24/2/469#B4-sensors-24-00469)]. Road bridge failures are attributed to a range of factors, including natural disasters, aging infrastructure, environmental conditions, deficiencies in structural design, material degradation due to environmental conditions, increased traffic loads, insufficient maintenance and inspection, and human error [[1](https://www.mdpi.com/1424-8220/24/2/469#B1-sensors-24-00469),[5](https://www.mdpi.com/1424-8220/24/2/469#B5-sensors-24-00469),[6](https://www.mdpi.com/1424-8220/24/2/469#B6-sensors-24-00469)]. 

Developing nations often face a myriad of challenges when it comes to bridge infrastructure development and monitoring. Some of the specific challenges include financial constraints; geopolitical and environmental factors; technology limitations; maintenance and upkeep; access to skilled labor; corruption and governance issues; inadequate planning; and, above all, impact on local sustained communities. Addressing these challenges requires a holistic approach involving technology development by academia and industry, government commitment, international support, improved governance, technology transfer, cap

# Monitoring Bridge Stability with IoT Sensors

![Writer: Avant]()

Traditionally, structural inspections have relied on visual assessments, manual measurements, and occasional testing. However, with advances in Internet of Things (IoT) technology, real-time monitoring systems have transformed how engineers assess and maintain bridge stability.

![Close-up of a bridge stability IoT sensor mounted on a yellow metal post on the side of a bridge, with a large truck crossing in the background. The sensor, covered in mud, monitors structural vibrations and stability, highlighting the rugged environment and use in New Zealand’s natural landscape.](https://static.wixstatic.com/media/0857ae_c3237623b2074cda9f3023f899240760~mv2.webp/v1/fill/w_147,h_82,al_c,q_80,usm_0.66_1.00_0.01,blur_2,enc_avif,quality_auto/0857ae_c3237623b2074cda9f3023f899240760~mv2.webp)

Real-time monitoring enables engineers to receive continuous updates on the structural health of bridges. This technology provides insights that are impossible to obtain with periodic inspections alone. For instance, an IoT system can detect subtle vibrations, shifts, or changes in the tilt angle of a bridge, often in response to environmental conditions or traffic loads. Early detection of these changes can help engineers address potential issues before they lead to serious structural problems.

### Types of Sensors Used in IoT-Based Bridge Monitoring

IoT-based bridge monitoring systems leverage a variety of sensors, each designed to measure specific parameters crucial to structural health. Here are some of the most common types:

**Vibration Sensors**

Vibration sensors measure oscillations within the structure, capturing data that reflects changes in load, stress, or environmental forces. By tracking vibration patterns over time, engineers can identify anomalies that might indicate potential structural weaknesses.

**Tilt Sensors**

Tilt sensors measure the angle of tilt along multiple axes (X, Y, and Z). These sensors are particularly useful for detecting shifts in a bridge's alignment, which could signal foundation issues, erosion, or other structural concerns.

**Strain Gauges**

Strain gauges measure the degree of deformation in bridge materials, providing data on stress distribution. This is essential for understanding how weight and environmental forces affect different parts of the structure.

**Temperature and Humidity Sensors**

Environmental sensors monitor temperature and humidity, which can impact material strength and bridge stability. For example, temperature fluctuations can cause expansion and contraction in bridge components, while humidity levels may influence corrosion rates.

![Weathered wooden bridge crossing a shallow river in a remote forested area. Surrounded by dense greenery, the bridge's worn structure is visible, highlighting the need for stability and vibration monitoring in isolated locations with minimal maintenance access.](https://static.wixstatic.com/media/0857ae_4f8cb82682f04561bf24e62bbc0317ee~mv2.webp/v1/fill/w_147,h_82,al_c,q_80,usm_0.66_1.00_0.01,blur_2,enc_avif,quality_auto/0857ae_4f8cb82682f04561bf24e62bbc0317ee~mv2.webp)

### Data Transmission and Communication Technologies

To function effectively, IoT sensors require reliable communication channels for data transmission. Two of the most commonly used technologies in bridge monitoring are:

**LoRaWAN (Long Range Wide Area Network)**

LoRaWAN technology is ideal for remote locations due to its long-range communication capabilities and low power requirements. LoRaWAN sensors can transmit data over several kilometers, making them suitable for monitoring bridges in rural or difficult-to-access areas.

**Cellular and 5G Networks**

For bridges in urban or highly trafficked areas, cellular networks, including emerging 5G, offer high-speed data transmission. This technology enables real-time data transfer, which is essential for high-frequency monitoring.

![Computer monitor displaying real-time data analytics for bridge stability monitoring. Various charts, graphs, and gauges show live information from IoT sensors, with a scenic view of rolling hills through the window in the background, emphasizing remote monitoring capability](https://static.wixstatic.com/media/0857ae_ca883bb715a04afb95716e44aa7e379e~mv2.webp/v1/fill/w_147,h_82,al_c,q_80,usm_0.66_1.00_0.01,blur_2,enc_avif,quality_auto/0857ae_ca883bb715a04afb95716e44aa7e379e~mv2.webp)

### Key Benefits of IoT Technology in Bridge Maintenance

Implementing IoT technology in bridge monitoring provides numerous benefits, including:

**Enhanced Data Accuracy**: Continuous data collection reduces human error and offers a more comprehensive understanding of structural behavior.

**Early Warning Systems**: Real-time alerts allow engineers to detect and address issues proactively, potentially preventing costly repairs and ensuring public safety.

**Cost Savings on Maintenance**: By automating data collection and analysis, IoT systems reduce the need for frequent manual inspections, leading to significant savings in maintenance budgets.

**Longer Asset Lifespan**: Through regular monitoring, minor issues can be addressed before they escalate, thereby extending the overall lifespan of bridge structures.

### Implementation Considerations for Civil Engineering Projects

When integrating IoT-based monitoring systems into civil engineering projects, several factors should be considered:

**Sensor Placement**

Strategic placement is critical to ensure that sensors capture accurate and relevant data. For example, placing vibration sensors near the center of the span may yield valuable information on load distribution, while strain gauges might be best placed on bridge supports.

**Power Supply and Battery Life**

Many IoT sensors operate on battery power. Engineers should choose devices with long battery life or consider solar-powered options, especially for remote installations.

**Data Management and Storage**

The vast amount of data generated by IoT sensors requires an efficient data management solution. Cloud-based platforms are commonly used for storing and analyzing data, allowing engineers to access information remotely.

**Integration with Existing Infrastructure**

Retrofitting existing structures with IoT sensors may require adjustments to accommodate wiring, mounting, and data transmission infrastructure. Ensuring compatibility with current systems is essential for seamless implementatio

![Drone hovering near a small, rural bridge over a dry riverbed in an open, golden landscape. The scene shows the use of aerial technology for bridge inspection and surveying in remote areas, with rolling hills in the background under a clear blue sky.](https://static.wixstatic.com/media/0857ae_c11bc0dcffd94ad6a5d7cade9018d60e~mv2.png/v1/fill/w_49,h_27,al_c,q_85,usm_0.66_1.00_0.01,blur_2,enc_avif,quality_auto/0857ae_c11bc0dcffd94ad6a5d7cade9018d60e~mv2.png)

### Challenges and Limitations of IoT-Based Monitoring Systems

While IoT technology offers substantial benefits for bridge monitoring, some challenges and limitations remain:

**Connectivity Issues**: In remote areas, maintaining a stable connection for data transmission can be challenging, especially when relying on cellular networks.

**Data Overload**: High-frequency monitoring can lead to data overload, making it necessary to implement filtering and prioritization mechanisms.

**Security Concerns**: IoT devices are vulnerable to cyberattacks. Ensuring data security and device integrity is essential to prevent unauthorized access.

**Initial Costs**: Implementing IoT technology may have high initial costs, including the purchase of sensors, installation, and data management solutions.

### 

IoT-based sensor technology is transforming bridge monitoring, offering civil engineers real-time insights and automated solutions for structural health assessments. While challenges remain, particularly in connectivity and security, the benefits make IoT monitoring a valuable tool in modern civil engineering.

Advantages of Structural Health Monitoring Using IoT and CAN bus Technology
===========================================================================

[New Products](https://www.phaseivengr.com/blog/category/new-products/ "New Products") ‧ [Technology Explainers](https://www.phaseivengr.com/blog/category/technology-explainers/ "Technology Explainers")

[Previous](https://www.phaseivengr.com/blog/phase-ivs-interview-with-industrial-iot-news/)

[Next](https://www.phaseivengr.com/blog/how-differential-air-pressure-gauge-sensors-are-used-in-manufacturing/)

![Image 4: SHM with Io](https://www.phaseivengr.com/wp-content/uploads/2022/03/SHM-with-Io.jpg.webp)

There has been a lot of news coverage lately about the troubles with US infrastructure. In fact, the [American Society for Civil Engineers 2021 Structural Report Card](https://infrastructurereportcard.org/) recently graded America’s infrastructure at a C-, mentioning “staggering maintenance backlogs,” with 11 of the 17 categories rated falling in the D range.

This is precisely why there is a growing need for a simple, reliable, and cost-effective system for remote structural health monitoring (SHM) using the Internet of Things (IoT), and Phase IV’s Leap Sensors® has the solution.

Structural Health of Infrastructure
-----------------------------------

![Image 6: Construction workers with bridge](https://www.phaseivengr.com/wp-content/uploads/2022/03/Construction-workers-with-bridge-shm-post-300x292.jpg.webp)

As buildings and structures age, safety becomes more of a concern as internal fatigue and degradation are often difficult to monitor and diagnose. Problems go unnoticed, often until it’s too late, resulting in costly and disruptive repairs. This is where structural health monitoring plays a key role. Traditionally, SHM needs to be performed manually by an engineer on-site. These manual measurements are only done every few years and are time and cost ineffective.

SHM using IoT is a breakthrough in every way. It allows for faster, more accurate, and in-depth monitoring of a structure on a regular basis. All the data from these improved measurements can be sent directly to monitoring software without an engineer ever having to go out in the field and inspect the structure.

SHM using IoT provides:

*   More consistent monitoring for a better understanding of how wear & tear impacts the structures.
*   Elimination of human error/failure to monitor.
*   Early prediction of possibly catastrophic failure.
*   A centrally saved and easily accessible record of data.

Examples of Structural Health Monitoring Sensors
------------------------------------------------

[There are a variety of sensors](https://www.phaseivengr.com/product-tag/structural-health-monitoring/) that can be used to ensure accurate remote monitoring of structures such as bridges, roads, embankments, overpasses, viaducts, railroads and more.These sensors include:

*   Strain sensors measure loads, compression, tension, bending, and torsion – as well as a wide variety of other strain indicators.
*   Crack meter sensors monitors linear crack propagation.
*   Inclinometer/tilt sensors monitor any shifting or collapsing of a foundation while embedded in soil in embankments or roadbeds.
*   Piezometers monitor fluid pressure or pore water in embankments, roadbeds, reservoirs, etc.

How Structural Health Monitoring with Wireless Strain Gauges Works
------------------------------------------------------------------

The sensor is integrated with the transceiver node, which both sends and receives information with the gateway. The software can be configured to send user-defined alerts based sensor readings – as well as storing data for reporting and analysis.

The most commonly used sensor for SHM is a [strain gauge sensor](https://www.phaseivengr.com/products/sensors/strain-gauge/). With consistent monitoring for small movements, deterioration can be detected to allow time to schedule maintenance as needed, and before it becomes a serious problem. Advances such as over-the-air zero-balancing have made wireless strain gauges much more accurate and easier to calibrate. In addition, increased battery life, transmission range, and convenient installation options including spot welding on-site all make wireless strain monitoring a practical option.

CAN bus Sensors in SHM
----------------------

One drawback to using wireless strain gauges in SHM has been the degradation of analog strain signal when sent over long cables.

Phase IV has leveraged CAN bus technology to “daisy chain” multiple strain gauge or load cell modules to one wireless Leap transceiver node. This makes it possible to monitor up to 60 points on structures such as beams, struts, bridges, overpasses, walls, etc. Edge computing at the module level converts the analog signal to a digital signal that can be passed along the long CAN bus cable without distortion of the readings.

The CAN bus system can also be used to combine sensor operations for various kinds of monitoring such as:

*   If there is an impact, the system will take strain and/or readings in multiple places.
*   If vibrations are detected above a certain value, the system can take several readings at critical points on the structure.
*   If the structure overheats, the sensor system can take strain readings at critical points.

Phase IV is the _only_ industrial wireless sensor provider to offer this breakthrough technology. Not only are we the only company to provide a completely wireless CAN bus system, but it can also easily integrate into and structure with wired SHM sensors, such as smart bridges- with ease and using the existing monitoring software.

![Image 8: wireless strain sensor remote structural health monitor beams](blob:http://localhost/f5c909c46bb5d2ef667042f8c68645c3)

SHM with IoT is the Future
--------------------------

Wireless SHM sensors are a powerful tool in civil engineering that allow for quick deployment and continuous monitoring of embankment creep, ground water, crack propagation, load monitoring, impact detection, weather monitoring, and strain. Leap Sensors provides an advanced, yet reliable system that makes it easy and cost-effective to monitor our critical infrastructure. One of our clients described it by saying, “It’s like x-ray vision combined with a crystal ball.”

[See our full range of SHM sensors.](https://www.phaseivengr.com/product-tag/structural-health-monitoring/)

Learn more about how Leap’s wireless sensor system can work for your SHM needs by [talking to one of our wireless sensor experts](https://www.phaseivengr.com/about-us/contact-us/) today.

enriched_points.expanded_content: IoT sensors provide real-time data on structural integrity, enabling early detection of anomalies, thereby enhancing overall safety and reliability of bridge infrastructure.
Continuous monitoring minimizes the need for frequent manual inspections, leading to significant savings in maintenance costs and allowing for timely repairs when necessary.
Proactive monitoring through IoT technology helps identify minor issues before they escalate, resulting in improved longevity and performance of bridge structures.
